title: Lunch on time
summary: On a Mumbai train platform, a journalist asks a dabbawala how a system with no app delivers two hundred thousand hot lunches a day.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Sir, I am from a newspaper. I want to write about your work. Is that okay?

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Yes, sit. The next train is in eight minutes. We can talk.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: How many lunches do you carry today?

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: I carry thirty-three. My team carries two hundred. The full system in Mumbai carries two hundred thousand a day.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Two hundred thousand hot lunches, from home to office, every day?

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Yes. Six days a week. We have done this for one hundred and thirty years.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: Do you use a computer? An app?

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: No app. We use a code on the lid of the box. Three or four letters and numbers. Each man on the way reads the code. He knows where the box goes next.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: But mistakes? Surely there are many.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: One in sixteen million. A study showed this. Sixteen million boxes — one mistake.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: That is better than a computer.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: It is not magic. It is small. It is repeating. Every man does one short part. Nobody does the whole trip.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Nobody owns it. Five thousand men own it together. We share the money. We share the work. The system has no boss. The boss is the train timetable.

body.speaker: Journalist
body.text: It is amazing. I do not understand how it works.

body.speaker: Ravi
body.text: Maybe that is why it works. If one man understood the whole thing, one man could break it. Now no one can.

annotations.meaningVi: mã / ký hiệu (ở đây: mã chữ-số trên nắp hộp)
thời gian biểu / bảng giờ tàu

grammarNotes.title: Numbers and frequency in description
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng nhiều số kết hợp với khoảng thời gian: 'two hundred thousand a day', 'six days a week', 'one hundred and thirty years', 'one in sixteen million'. Cấu trúc 'X + a + đơn vị thời gian' = X mỗi (ngày/tuần/năm). Số ghép với 'and' trước hàng chục: 'one hundred and thirty', không 'one hundred thirty' trong tiếng Anh-Anh.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The dialogue uses numbers with time units: 'two hundred thousand a day', 'six days a week', 'one hundred and thirty years', 'one in sixteen million'. 'X + a + time unit' means X per (day/week/year). British English uses 'and' before tens: 'one hundred and thirty', not 'one hundred thirty'.

translationVi:
Phóng viên: Anh ơi, tôi ở báo. Tôi muốn viết về công việc của anh. Có được không?
Ravi: Được, ngồi đi. Tám phút nữa có chuyến tàu. Mình nói chuyện được.
Phóng viên: Hôm nay anh mang bao nhiêu hộp cơm?
Ravi: Tôi mang ba mươi ba. Nhóm tôi mang hai trăm. Cả hệ thống ở Mumbai mang hai trăm nghìn hộp một ngày.
Phóng viên: Hai trăm nghìn hộp cơm nóng, từ nhà tới công sở, mỗi ngày?
Ravi: Vâng. Sáu ngày một tuần. Chúng tôi làm việc này một trăm ba mươi năm rồi.
Phóng viên: Các anh dùng máy tính không? Ứng dụng?
Ravi: Không ứng dụng. Chúng tôi dùng mã trên nắp hộp. Ba hoặc bốn ký tự. Mỗi người dọc đường đọc mã. Anh ta biết hộp đi đâu kế tiếp.
Phóng viên: Nhưng nhầm thì sao? Chắc nhiều nhầm lắm.
Ravi: Một trên mười sáu triệu. Có nghiên cứu cho thấy. Mười sáu triệu hộp — một lần nhầm.
Phóng viên: Tốt hơn máy tính.
Ravi: Không phải phép màu. Là nhỏ. Là lặp đi lặp lại. Mỗi người làm một đoạn ngắn. Không ai làm cả hành trình.
Phóng viên: Ai sở hữu hệ thống này?
Ravi: Không ai sở hữu. Năm nghìn người sở hữu cùng nhau. Chúng tôi chia tiền. Chúng tôi chia việc. Hệ thống không có sếp. Sếp là bảng giờ tàu.
Phóng viên: Tuyệt vời quá. Tôi không hiểu nó vận hành thế nào.
Ravi: Có lẽ vì thế nó vận hành. Nếu một người hiểu hết, một người có thể phá nó. Bây giờ không ai phá được.

questions.prompt: How many lunches does the whole Mumbai system carry per day?
questions.explanation: 'The full system in Mumbai carries two hundred thousand a day.'

questions.prompt: How many does Ravi personally carry?
How long has the dabbawala system existed?
questions.explanation: 'We have done this for one hundred and thirty years.'

options: A short code of letters and numbers on the lid
A barcode scanned by a phone app
questions.explanation: 'We use a code on the lid of the box. Three or four letters and numbers.'

questions.prompt: How does Ravi describe the secret of the system?
options: Each man does one short part — nobody does the whole trip
Everyone does the same single long route
A central manager assigns each box
questions.explanation: 'Every man does one short part. Nobody does the whole trip.'

options: Five thousand men together — nobody alone owns it

questions.prompt: Who is the 'boss' of the system?
Why does Ravi suggest the system works precisely because no one understands all of it?
options: If one man understood the whole thing, one man could break it
Reliable systems can be built from many small, repeated, shared actions — not only from one centre or one app
Newspapers should write only about technology

cloze.template:
Journalist: Sir, I am from a {{b1}}. I want to write about your work. Is that okay?
Ravi: Yes, sit. The next train is in eight minutes. We can talk.
Journalist: How many {{b2}} do you carry today?
Ravi: I carry thirty-three. My team carries two hundred. The full system in Mumbai carries two hundred {{b3}} a day.
Journalist: Two hundred thousand hot lunches, from home to office, every day?
Ravi: Yes. Six days a week. We have done this for one hundred and {{b4}} years.
Journalist: Do you use a computer? An app?
Ravi: No app. We use a {{b5}} on the lid of the box. Three or four letters and numbers. Each man on the way reads the code. He knows where the box goes next.
Journalist: But mistakes? Surely there are many.
Ravi: One in {{b6}} million. A study showed this. Sixteen million boxes — one mistake.
Journalist: That is better than a computer.
Ravi: It is not magic. It is small. It is repeating. Every man does one short {{b7}}. Nobody does the whole trip.
Journalist: Who owns this system?
Ravi: Nobody owns it. Five thousand men own it {{b8}}. We share the money. We share the work. The system has no {{b9}}. The boss is the train timetable.
Journalist: It is amazing. I do not understand how it works.
Ravi: Maybe that is why it works. If one man understood the whole thing, one man could {{b10}} it. Now no one can.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Ravi says the system works partly because no single person understands the whole of it. Think of a system in your life — at work, at school, in your family — that fails because one person owns too much of it. Could it survive better if it were broken into 'small, repeating' parts? What would have to change?
